---
title: Interactive Dashboards with Quarto
categories: [quarto, dashboards, data-visualization, shiny, lecture, intermediate]
description: "Covers building interactive Quarto Dashboards with value boxes, Plotly charts, and DT tables, plus optional Shiny reactivity and deployment options."
---

<!--
Ideal prerequisite lessons:
- Intro to literate analysis with R and Quarto
- Interactivity with Shiny Apps
-->

:::{.callout-learning}
After completing this session, you will be able to:

- Describe what a Quarto Dashboard is and where it fits in the data product landscape
- Configure the YAML header to render a `.qmd` file as a Quarto Dashboard
- Organize dashboard layout using markdown heading levels and attributes
- Add value boxes, interactive charts, and interactive tables to a dashboard
- Customize a dashboard's appearance using built-in themes
- Publish a static Quarto Dashboard
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## Acknowledgments

This lesson draws from the [Quarto Dashboards documentation](https://quarto.org/docs/dashboards/),
and uses data from the [Yolo Bypass Fish Monitoring
Program](https://doi.org/10.6073/pasta/b0b15aef7f3b52d2c5adc10004c05a6f),
collected by the Interagency Ecological Program.
:::

## Overview

Using Shiny, you can build fully reactive apps powered by a live R server. Sometimes, though, you
want something easier to build and share. Imagine a dashboard layout with basic interactive charts
and tables, rendered once and published as a static web page. That is exactly what Quarto Dashboards
offer.

In this session, we will build a monitoring dashboard for the Yolo Bypass fish dataset from
scratch. By the end, you will have a multi-page, themed, interactive dashboard and know how
to publish it.

::: {.callout-important}
## Quarto version requirement

Quarto Dashboards require **Quarto 1.4 or later**. Check your version by running `quarto check` in the terminal. Download the latest version at [quarto.org/docs/download](https://quarto.org/docs/download/).
:::

## Where Dashboards Fit

At a high level, here is where Quarto Dashboards fit into the data product landscape:

| Product | What it is | Interactivity |
|---------|------------|---------------|
| Script or notebook | R code rendered once | None (static output) |
| Quarto document | Reproducible report as HTML or PDF | None (static output) |
| Quarto doc + widgets | Same, with JS-based widgets embedded | Limited (hover, zoom, sort on pre-rendered data) |
| **Quarto Dashboard** | **Card-based layout with JS widgets** | **Limited (same browser-side interactivity, in a purpose-built layout)** |
| **Quarto Dashboard + Shiny** | **Dashboard layout with a live R server** | **Full (users control computations)** |
| Shiny app | Standalone reactive application | Full (users control everything) |

Dashboards occupy a sweet spot: they look like dashboards and support a rich layout, but
require no server to host. Interactive charts (Plotly) and tables (DT) run entirely in the
reader's browser.

### When to use a dashboard vs. Shiny {.unnumbered}

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    A[Goal: share data interactively] --> B{Does it need<br>reactivity from R?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Shiny or<br>Dashboard + Shiny]
    B -- No --> D{Is a card-based layout<br>with browser widgets enough?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Quarto Dashboard<br>static]
    D -- No --> F[Shiny app]
    E --> G[Easy to build<br>Host anywhere -- no server needed]
    C --> H[Requires Posit Connect Cloud<br>or shinyapps.io]
    F --> H
```

### Real-world examples {.unnumbered}

- [New Zealand Earthquakes](https://pub.demo.posit.team/public/nz-quakes/quakes.html) -- spatial data with interactive map
- [Housing Market at a Glance](https://ivelasq.github.io/mortgage-dashboard/) -- multi-page layout with value boxes and maps
- [Diamonds Data](https://jjallaire.shinyapps.io/diamonds/) -- adds Shiny inputs for full reactivity

## Dashboard Anatomy

A Quarto Dashboard is a `.qmd` file with `format: dashboard` in its YAML header. Everything else
uses the Quarto markdown you already know, but now the heading hierarchy takes on a special meaning
for layout:

| Markdown | Dashboard meaning |
|----------|-------------------|
| `#` Level 1 heading | A new **page** (shown in the navigation bar) |
| `##` Level 2 heading | A new **row** (within a page) |
| Code chunk | A **card** (column within the current row) |

![](images/quarto_dashboards_reports/dashboard-structure.png){.lightbox fig-alt="Annotated side-by-side view of a Quarto dashboard: the QMD source (left) and rendered output (right) are color-coded to show that the YAML format is 'dashboard', H1 (#) headings define pages, H2 (##) headings define rows, and each code chunk within a row creates a column card" fig-cap="Dashboard layout: each `##` heading is a row; each code chunk within a row is a column card."}

### Setting up the YAML {.unnumbered}

Like any Quarto document, we need a YAML header specifying metadata and options. For a dashboard,
the only required option is `format: dashboard`.

Here is an example of minimum YAML for a dashboard:

```yaml
---
title: "My Dashboard"
format: dashboard
---
```

And here is more complete YAML with theming, which we'll discuss more later:

```yaml
---
title: "Delta Fish Monitoring"
author: "Your Name"
format:
  dashboard:
    theme: cosmo
    orientation: rows
---
```

`orientation: rows` is the default (so `##` headings create rows). Setting
`orientation: columns` flips it so `##` headings create columns.

### Layout attributes {.unnumbered}

Control relative sizes by adding attributes to headings:

```markdown
## Row {height="30%"}

## Row {height="70%"}
```

Create tabsets within a row with the `.tabset` class:

```markdown
## Row {.tabset}
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Heading text is discarded

Dashboard headings define _layout_ only. The heading text itself (e.g., `Row` in the examples above)
is not displayed in the rendered output. Use whatever text helps you navigate your source code.
:::

Here is a quick reference for some useful layout attributes:

| Attribute | Where to use | Effect |
|-----------|-------------|--------|
| `{height="30%"}` | `##` row heading | Sets relative row height on the page |
| `{width="40%"}` | `###` column heading | Sets relative column width within a row |
| `{.tabset}` | `##` row or `###` column | Converts cards into tabs |
| `{.sidebar}` | `##` heading | Creates an input sidebar (for Shiny integration) |
| `{.hidden}` | `#` page heading | Excludes a page from the navigation bar |
| `orientation: columns` | YAML | Makes `##` headings into columns instead of rows |

::: {.callout-caution appearance="minimal"}
Add multiple attributes within one set of curly braces, separated by a space:
`## Row {height="30%" .tabset}`
:::

## Dashboard Components

### Value Boxes {.unnumbered}

Value boxes display a single key metric with a title, icon, and color. They are a natural
fit for summary statistics at the top of a dashboard.

Use the `#| content: valuebox` chunk option and return a named `list()` with three fields:

```{r}
#| label: valuebox-syntax
#| eval: false
#| content: valuebox
#| title: "Species Monitored"
list(
  icon = "binoculars",
  color = "primary",
  value = 4
)
```

- **`icon`**: a [Bootstrap Icon](https://icons.getbootstrap.com/) name -- search the gallery by keyword (e.g., `"water"`, `"binoculars"`, `"bar-chart"`)
- **`color`**: a Bootstrap role (`primary`, `secondary`, `success`, `info`, `warning`, `danger`) or a hex string like `"#2C7BB6"`
- **`value`**: any R object rendered as text -- a number, formatted string, etc.

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Custom icons

Bootstrap Icons covers 2,000+ icons; searching by keyword (try "water", "arrow-up-circle", "clipboard-data") finds something usable in most cases. If you genuinely need a custom image or agency logo, you can pass an SVG string via `htmltools::HTML('<svg ...>...</svg>')` as the `icon` value -- see the [value box documentation](https://quarto.org/docs/dashboards/data-display.html#value-boxes) for details and caveats.
:::

### Interactive Charts {.unnumbered}

Any R graphics output works as a dashboard card, including `ggplot2`, base R, and `lattice`.
For interactive charts, [`plotly`](https://plotly.com/r/) is the simplest option:
`ggplotly()` converts any `ggplot2` plot into an interactive Plotly chart.

```{r}
#| label: plotly-syntax
#| eval: false
library(ggplot2)
library(plotly)

p <- ggplot(fish, aes(x = SampleDate, y = Secchi)) +
  geom_point(color = "steelblue", alpha = 0.4) +
  labs(x = "Date", y = "Secchi depth (m)") +
  theme_light()

ggplotly(p)    # <1>
```
1. `ggplotly()` wraps any ggplot -- no other changes needed

### Interactive Tables {.unnumbered}

Use `DT::datatable()` for interactive, sortable, searchable tables:

```{r}
#| label: dt-syntax
#| eval: false
library(DT)

catch_summary %>%
  datatable(
    rownames = FALSE,
    options = list(pageLength = 10)
  )
```

For simple static tables, `knitr::kable()` works fine and renders faster.

## Building a Dashboard

Now let us build a Delta Fish Monitoring dashboard from scratch using the Yolo Bypass dataset.

### Setup: Create the file {.unnumbered}

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Positron

1. Go to **File → New File → Quarto Document** and save it as `delta-dashboard.qmd` in your project folder.
2. Replace the default YAML with the dashboard YAML below.

#### RStudio

1. Go to **File → New File → Quarto Document...**, give it a title, click **Create**, and save as `delta-dashboard.qmd`.
2. Replace the default YAML with the dashboard YAML below.

:::

Start with this YAML at the top of the file:

```yaml
---
title: "Delta Fish Monitoring"
format:
  dashboard:
    theme: cosmo
---
```

Then add a chunk right after the YAML to load packages. Here we use a hash-pipe `label` option to
label this chunk. The label will _not_ be appear in the dashboard output, but it enables automatic
outline-based navigation of your document in IDEs like Positron.

```{{r}}
#| label: load packages

library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(lubridate)
library(plotly)
library(DT)
```

Next add a chunk for loading data. Rather than simply loading from the URL, we apply an `if`/`else`
caching pattern to download the data file only if it hasn't already been locally cached.

```{{r}}
#| label: load data

# Set paths
yolo_catch_url <- "https://pasta.lternet.edu/package/data/eml/edi/233/2/015e494911cf35c90089ced5a3127334"
data_dir <- "data"
yolo_catch_file <- file.path(data_dir, "yolo_catch.csv")

# Download data if not already cached
if (!file.exists(yolo_catch_file)) {
  message("Downloading file...")
  dir.create(data_dir, showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)
  download.file(
    url = yolo_catch_url,
    destfile = yolo_catch_file
  )
  message("Saved to: ", yolo_catch_file)
} else {
  message("Using cached file.")
}

# Load the (locally cached) data
fish <- read_csv(yolo_catch_file, guess_max = 100000) %>%
  mutate(SampleDate = mdy(SampleDate)) %>%
  filter(grepl("Salmon|Striped Bass|Smelt|Sturgeon", CommonName))
```

<!--
library(contentid)
sha1 <- "hash://sha1/317d7f840e598f5f3be732ab0e04f00a8051c6d0"
yolo_catch_file <- contentid::resolve(sha1, registries = c("dataone"), store = TRUE)
-->

::: {.callout-tip}
## Tip: render early and often

After each incremental change to the Quarto document, render the file (Ctrl/Cmd+Shift+K or the
**Render** button) and open the result in a browser to see the layout take shape. Catching layout
issues early is much easier than debugging them at the end!
:::

### Step 1: Add a page and value boxes {.unnumbered}

After the setup chunk, add a Level 1 heading to create the first page, a row heading with a
height attribute, and three value box chunks:

````{verbatim}
#| lang: markdown
# Summary

## Row {height="25%"}

```{r}
#| content: valuebox
#| title: "Species Monitored"
list(icon = "binoculars", color = "primary",
     value = n_distinct(fish$CommonName))
```

```{r}
#| content: valuebox
#| title: "Years of Data"
list(icon = "calendar3", color = "success",
     value = n_distinct(year(fish$SampleDate)))
```

```{r}
#| content: valuebox
#| title: "Total Observations"
list(icon = "table", color = "info",
     value = nrow(fish))
```
````

The `#` heading creates a "Summary" page in the navigation bar. Three code chunks in the
same row produce three side-by-side columns automatically.

::: {.callout-note}
## `height` is relative, not absolute

`{height="25%"}` sets the *proportion* of vertical space this row gets relative to other
rows on the same page. With only one row defined so far, it expands to fill the whole
screen -- that is normal. The height constraint only takes effect in Step 2 once a second
row exists to share the space with.
:::

Render the file. You should see a single page with three value boxes filling the screen.

![](images/quarto_dashboards_reports/dashboard-valueboxes.png){.lightbox fig-alt="Three colored value boxes showing dataset summary statistics: 'Species Monitored: 7' (blue), 'Years of Data: 21' (green), and 'Total Observations: 21406' (purple)" fig-cap="Rendered value boxes summarizing the Yolo Bypass dataset."}

### Step 2: Add an interactive chart {.unnumbered}

Add a second row below the value boxes with a Plotly time series:

````{verbatim}
#| lang: markdown
## Row {height="75%"}

```{r}
#| title: "Water Clarity Over Time"
p <- fish %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = SampleDate, y = Secchi)) +
    geom_point(color = "#2C7BB6", alpha = 0.4) +
    labs(x = "Date", y = "Secchi depth (m)") +
    theme_light()

ggplotly(p)
```
````

The card title comes from `#| title:` -- it appears as the card header in the dashboard.
The two rows now sum to 100% of the page height (25% + 75%).

![](images/quarto_dashboards_reports/dashboard-plotly.png){.lightbox fig-alt="Interactive Plotly scatter plot titled 'Water Clarity Over Time' showing Secchi depth in meters on the y-axis and date on the x-axis, with data points from approximately 1998 to 2019 clustered mostly below 0.4 m" fig-cap="Plotly scatter plot of water clarity rendered in a dashboard card."}

::: {.callout-note}
## Exercise: Show a static plot instead

Notice the difference if you remove the `ggplotly()` call, and just return `p` as a plain `ggplot2`
object instead.
:::

### Step 3: Add an interactive table {.unnumbered}

Add a second chunk inside the same `## Row {height="75%"}` block to put a table alongside
the chart:

````{verbatim}
#| lang: markdown
```{r}
#| title: "Catch by Species"
fish %>%
  group_by(CommonName) %>%
  summarize(
    Observations = n(),
    `Total Catch` = sum(Count, na.rm = TRUE),
    `Avg Secchi (m)` = round(mean(Secchi, na.rm = TRUE), 2)
  ) %>%
  datatable(rownames = FALSE, options = list(pageLength = 5))
```
````

Multiple chunks in the same row produce side-by-side columns. Quarto divides the row width
equally by default.

![](images/quarto_dashboards_reports/dashboard-datatable.png){.lightbox fig-alt="Interactive DT table titled 'Catch by Species' showing 7 fish species with columns for Observations, Total Catch, and Avg Secchi (m); Chinook Salmon has 10,238 observations and Striped Bass has the highest total catch at 46,371" fig-cap="DT data table of catch totals by species rendered in a dashboard card."}

::: {.callout-note}
## Exercise: Show a static table instead

Notice the difference if you remove the `datatable()` call, and instead format the data frame as a
static HTML table using `knitr::kable()`.
:::

### Step 4: Add a second page {.unnumbered}

Add a new `#` heading below everything to create a second page:

````{verbatim}
#| lang: markdown
# Catch Trends

## Row

```{r}
#| title: "Annual Catch by Species"
annual_catch <- fish %>%
  mutate(Year = year(SampleDate)) %>%
  group_by(Year, CommonName) %>%
  summarize(total_catch = sum(Count, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

p2 <- annual_catch %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Year, y = total_catch, color = CommonName)) +
    geom_line() +
    geom_point() +
    labs(x = "Year", y = "Total Catch", color = "Species") +
    theme_light()

ggplotly(p2)
```
````

The navigation bar now shows two pages: Summary and Catch Trends.

![](images/quarto_dashboards_reports/dashboard-page2.png){.lightbox fig-alt="Screenshot of a Quarto dashboard's 'Catch Trends' page showing a line chart of annual total catch by species from 1997 to 2019. Chinook Salmon and Striped Bass have the highest and most variable catch totals; the remaining species (Delta Smelt, Longfin Smelt, White Sturgeon, etc.) remain near zero throughout" fig-cap="The 'Catch Trends' page with an annual catch line chart by species."}

### Step 5: Add tabsets {.unnumbered}

The Catch Trends page currently shows the annual chart as a single full-page card. Tabsets
let you pack additional content into the same row: add `.tabset` to the `## Row` heading and
each code chunk becomes a separate tab.

Go back to the `# Catch Trends` page you added in Step 4 and replace its content with the following
code; the only change to the existing row is adding `.tabset`, plus a second chunk for a raw data
table:

````{verbatim}
#| lang: markdown
# Catch Trends

## Row {.tabset}

```{r}
#| title: "By Year"
annual_catch <- fish %>%
  mutate(Year = year(SampleDate)) %>%
  group_by(Year, CommonName) %>%
  summarize(total_catch = sum(Count, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

p2 <- annual_catch %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Year, y = total_catch, color = CommonName)) +
    geom_line() +
    geom_point() +
    labs(x = "Year", y = "Total Catch", color = "Species") +
    theme_light()

ggplotly(p2)
```

```{r}
#| title: "Raw Data"
fish %>%
  select(SampleDate, CommonName, Count, Secchi) %>%
  datatable(rownames = FALSE)
```
````

Render again. The Catch Trends page should now show two tabs: the interactive line chart and
a browseable raw data table.

![](images/quarto_dashboards_reports/dashboard-page2tab2.png){.lightbox fig-alt="Screenshot of the 'Catch Trends' page with the 'Raw Data' tab active, showing a searchable DT table of 21,406 entries with columns SampleDate, CommonName, Count, and Secchi" fig-cap="The 'Raw Data' tab on the Catch Trends page showing the full browseable dataset."}

### Putting it all together {.unnumbered}

If you've been following along this whole time, you should now have a dashboard that looks something
like this:

![](images/quarto_dashboards_reports/dashboard-full-page1.png){.lightbox fig-alt="Screenshot of the completed 'Summary' page of the Delta Fish Monitoring dashboard, with three value boxes at the top (Species Monitored: 7, Years of Data: 21, Total Observations: 21,406) and two cards below: a water clarity scatter plot on the left and a catch-by-species data table on the right" fig-cap="The completed Summary page of the Delta Fish Monitoring dashboard."}

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Complete delta-dashboard.qmd

````{verbatim}
#| lang: markdown
---
title: "Delta Fish Monitoring"
format:
  dashboard:
    theme: cosmo
---

```{r}
#| label: load packages

library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(lubridate)
library(plotly)
library(DT)
```

```{r}
#| label: load data

# Set paths
yolo_catch_url <- "https://pasta.lternet.edu/package/data/eml/edi/233/2/015e494911cf35c90089ced5a3127334"
data_dir <- "data"
yolo_catch_file <- file.path(data_dir, "yolo_catch.csv")

# Download data if not already cached
if (!file.exists(yolo_catch_file)) {
  message("Downloading file...")
  dir.create(data_dir, showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)
  download.file(
    url = yolo_catch_url,
    destfile = yolo_catch_file
  )
  message("Saved to: ", yolo_catch_file)
} else {
  message("Using cached file.")
}

# Load the (locally cached) data
fish <- read_csv(yolo_catch_file, guess_max = 100000) %>%
  mutate(SampleDate = mdy(SampleDate)) %>%
  filter(grepl("Salmon|Striped Bass|Smelt|Sturgeon", CommonName))
```

# Summary

## Row {height="25%"}

```{r}
#| content: valuebox
#| title: "Species Monitored"
list(icon = "binoculars", color = "primary",
     value = n_distinct(fish$CommonName))
```

```{r}
#| content: valuebox
#| title: "Years of Data"
list(icon = "calendar3", color = "success",
     value = n_distinct(year(fish$SampleDate)))
```

```{r}
#| content: valuebox
#| title: "Total Observations"
list(icon = "table", color = "info",
     value = nrow(fish))
```

## Row {height="75%"}

```{r}
#| title: "Water Clarity Over Time"
p <- fish %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = SampleDate, y = Secchi)) +
    geom_point(color = "#2C7BB6", alpha = 0.4) +
    labs(x = "Date", y = "Secchi depth (m)") +
    theme_light()

ggplotly(p)
```

```{r}
#| title: "Catch by Species"
fish %>%
  group_by(CommonName) %>%
  summarize(
    Observations = n(),
    `Total Catch` = sum(Count, na.rm = TRUE),
    `Avg Secchi (m)` = round(mean(Secchi, na.rm = TRUE), 2)
  ) %>%
  datatable(rownames = FALSE, options = list(pageLength = 5))
```

# Catch Trends

## Row {.tabset}

```{r}
#| title: "By Year"
annual_catch <- fish %>%
  mutate(Year = year(SampleDate)) %>%
  group_by(Year, CommonName) %>%
  summarize(total_catch = sum(Count, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

p2 <- annual_catch %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Year, y = total_catch, color = CommonName)) +
    geom_line() +
    geom_point() +
    labs(x = "Year", y = "Total Catch", color = "Species") +
    theme_light()

ggplotly(p2)
```

```{r}
#| title: "Raw Data"
fish %>%
  select(SampleDate, CommonName, Count, Secchi) %>%
  datatable(rownames = FALSE)
```
````
:::

## Applying a Theme

Change the `theme:` in your YAML to any of the available Bootstrap themes:

```yaml
format:
  dashboard:
    theme: flatly
```

Available themes: `cerulean`, `cosmo`, `cyborg`, `darkly`, `flatly`, `journal`,
`lumen`, `lux`, `minty`, `pulse`, `sandstone`, `simplex`, `sketchy`, `slate`, `solar`,
`spacelab`, `superhero`, `united`, `vapor`, `yeti`, `zephyr`.

Re-render to see the change immediately. Themes like `flatly`, `lumen`, and `cosmo` tend to look
professional and clean. For a dramatic alternative, try `darkly`.

Browse the full gallery and explore further customization options at
[quarto.org/docs/dashboards/theming.html](https://quarto.org/docs/dashboards/theming.html).

## Your Turn

::: {.callout-note}
## Exercise: Extend the Delta Dashboard

Working on your `delta-dashboard.qmd`, complete the following tasks:

1. **Add a value box** that shows the date range of the data (e.g., "1998 - 2018").
   - Hint: extract the year with `format(min(fish$SampleDate), "%Y")`
   - Combine two year strings with `paste0(start_year, " - ", end_year)`

2. **Add a smoothed trend line** to the Secchi depth scatter plot.
   - Add `geom_smooth(method = "loess", color = "firebrick")` to the ggplot before calling `ggplotly()`

3. **Add a third page** with a visualization or summary of your choosing. Some ideas:
   - A bar chart of total catch by species across all years
   - A histogram of Secchi depth values
   - A summary table of the 10 most recent observations

4. **Try two different themes** and pick the one you prefer. Change `theme:` in your YAML and re-render each time.

5. **Revise the layout** by converting the main chart row of the Summary page to a tabset showing the Secchi chart and the catch table as tabs instead of side by side.
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## Tips

- Bootstrap Icons gallery: [icons.getbootstrap.com](https://icons.getbootstrap.com/) -- search by keyword (try "water", "arrow-up", "bar-chart")
- Value box color options: `primary`, `secondary`, `success`, `info`, `warning`, `danger`
- Quarto Dashboard theming reference: [quarto.org/docs/dashboards/theming.html](https://quarto.org/docs/dashboards/theming.html)
:::

## Bonus: Adding Shiny Interactivity

Static dashboards cover a lot of ground, especially with interactive components like `plotly`
figures and `DT` tables. However, if you need reactive user input such as filtering by date,
selecting a species, or re-running a model, you can layer Shiny into a Quarto Dashboard.

Add `server: shiny` to the YAML:

```yaml
---
title: "My Reactive Dashboard"
format: dashboard
server: shiny
---
```

Then label your data-loading chunk with `context: setup` (runs once on startup), use
`## Sidebar {.sidebar}` to create an input panel, place an output placeholder in the body, and
fill it in a `context: server` chunk:

````{verbatim}
#| lang: markdown
```{r}
#| context: setup
fish <- read.csv(...)
```

## Sidebar {.sidebar}

```{r}
sliderInput("date_range", "Date range:", ...)
```

## Row

```{r}
plotOutput("my_plot")  # UI: reserves the card slot and registers an ID
```

```{r}
#| context: server
output$my_plot <- renderPlot({ ... })  # server: reactively fills the slot
```
````

::: {.callout-note}
Shiny dashboards require hosting where an R session can run -- Posit Connect Cloud
(connect.posit.cloud), shinyapps.io, a Shiny server, or your own computer just for internal purposes
-- just like standalone Shiny apps. Consequently, they don't have the same ease of deployment as
plain Quarto dashboards.
:::

### Applying this to the Delta Dashboard {.unnumbered}

Here is exactly what to change in `delta-dashboard.qmd` to add and use a species multiselect.
Step 4 below makes only one of the charts reactive to the multiselect input, but you can follow a
similar pattern to make other charts and tables reactive too.

**1. YAML**: Add `server: shiny`.

```yaml
---
title: "Delta Fish Monitoring"
format:
  dashboard:
    theme: cosmo
server: shiny
---
```

**2. Setup chunk**: Add `context: setup` to both the package and data loading chunks so that these
happen only once at startup rather than once per user interaction. Here is an excerpted example for
the package loading chunk:

````{verbatim}
#| lang: markdown
```{r}
#| label: load packages
#| context: setup

library(readr)
...
```
````

**3. Global sidebar**: Add `## Sidebar {.sidebar}` before the first `# ` page heading so the
input appears on every page. Within this chunk, we'll add a Shiny multiselect widget.

````{verbatim}
#| lang: markdown
## Sidebar {.sidebar}

```{r}
checkboxGroupInput(
  inputId = "species",
  label = "Select species:",
  choices  = sort(unique(fish$CommonName)),
  selected = sort(unique(fish$CommonName))
)
```
````

**4. Reactive charts**: Each reactive output requires a pair of chunks -- one for the UI and one for
the server. This means splitting up the single chunk we used in the ordinary dashboard document and
adding the Shiny functions.

The **UI chunk** uses an `*Output` function to reserve space in the card, add the UI label, and name
the output slot:

````{verbatim}
#| lang: markdown
```{r}
#| title: "Annual Catch by Species"
plotlyOutput("annual_catch_plot")
```
````

The **server chunk** uses a `render*` function to fill the output slot reactively whenever
`input$species` (from our widget in the sidebar) changes.
Don't forget to add `filter(CommonName %in% input$species)` to the data pipeline in this chunk!

````{verbatim}
#| lang: markdown
```{r}
#| context: server
output$annual_catch_plot <- renderPlotly({
  annual_catch <- fish %>%
    filter(CommonName %in% input$species) %>%
    mutate(Year = year(SampleDate)) %>%
    group_by(Year, CommonName) %>%
    summarize(total_catch = sum(Count, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

  p2 <- annual_catch %>%
    ggplot(aes(x = Year, y = total_catch, color = CommonName)) +
      geom_line() +
      geom_point() +
      labs(x = "Year", y = "Total Catch", color = "Species") +
      theme_light()

  ggplotly(p2)
})
```
````

::: {.callout-note}
## Why two chunks?

In a Quarto Dashboard with `server: shiny`, all reactive outputs require the explicit UI/server
split: a UI chunk with the output placeholder created by calling `*Output()` with some `id`, and a
`context: server` chunk that assigns to `output$id` using a corresponding `render*()` function.

| Output type | UI placeholder | Server renderer |
|-------------|----------------|-----------------|
| ggplot2 | `plotOutput("id")` | `renderPlot({})` |
| Plotly | `plotlyOutput("id")` | `renderPlotly({})` |
| DT table | `DTOutput("id")` | `renderDT({})` |
| Value box | `valueBoxOutput("id")` | `renderValueBox({})` |
| Arbitrary HTML | `uiOutput("id")` | `renderUI({})` |

Calling `renderPlot()` or `renderPlotly()` inline without a matching output placeholder produces a
blank card.
:::

That is the whole pattern! Chunks that don't use the fish data (or that you simply don't need to be
reactive) can stay unchanged.

## Deploying Your Dashboard

Once your dashboard renders correctly, there are several ways to share it. Static dashboards
(no Shiny) are easiest to host because they are just HTML files.

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### GitHub Pages

If you already have a GitHub repository, publish the rendered HTML to GitHub Pages using
the same approach covered in the Quarto Websites session.

1. Render locally: `quarto render delta-dashboard.qmd`
2. Commit the generated `delta-dashboard.html` to your repository
3. Enable GitHub Pages in the repository settings

#### Posit Connect Cloud

[Posit Connect Cloud](https://connect.posit.cloud/) is a free hosting service from Posit
for Quarto content and Shiny apps. Create an account, then publish directly from the
terminal:

```bash
quarto publish connect-cloud delta-dashboard.qmd
```

Follow the prompts to authenticate in your browser. Your dashboard will be assigned a
public URL under `connect.posit.cloud`. Posit Connect Cloud handles both **static
dashboards** and **Shiny dashboards** (with `server: shiny`).

#### shinyapps.io

[shinyapps.io](https://www.shinyapps.io/) is an older Posit service that also hosts Shiny
dashboards. If your dashboard uses `server: shiny` and you already have a shinyapps.io
account, publish using the Publish button or the `rsconnect` package:

::: {.panel-tabset}
##### Positron

Click the **Publish** icon (cloud with arrow) in the Viewer toolbar after running the app,
or run `rsconnect::deployApp("path/to/your/dashboard")` in the Console.

##### RStudio

Click the blue **Publish** button in the top-right corner of the running app window.
:::

See the [Shiny session](s05_shiny_introduction.qmd) for full account setup instructions.

:::

## Resources

- [Quarto Dashboards documentation](https://quarto.org/docs/dashboards/) is the official reference for all layout options, components, and theming
- [Quarto Dashboard layout guide](https://quarto.org/docs/dashboards/layout.html) shows detailed layout options with examples
- [Quarto theming reference](https://quarto.org/docs/dashboards/theming.html) describes dashboard color and font options
- [Bootstrap Icons gallery](https://icons.getbootstrap.com/) is a searchable icon library for value boxes
- [plotly for R](https://plotly.com/r/) is an interactive chart library that integrates with
  `ggplot2`
- [DT package documentation](https://rstudio.github.io/DT/) shows how to create interactive HTML tables using R
